input_path: data\papers\Ramdhan et al. - 2025 - Engineering Automotive Digital Twins on Standardized Architectures A Case Study.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study: 1/10‑scale autonomous vehicle platform used for evaluating Adaptive Cruise Control (ACC). The physical twin consists of a teleoperated vehicle retrofitted with advanced sensor and control technology, featuring lower‑ and upper‑level chassis modules and integrated autonomy elements (sensors, ECUs, control units). It operates in the automotive domain, performing ACC tasks such as lead‑vehicle detection, desired‑speed computation, and vehicle‑state tracking. The system’s resource‑constrained ECUs and requirement for real‑time, safety‑critical data make it an ideal candidate for a digital twin that can offload computations, provide remote oversight, and enable detailed telemetry.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services: The Digital Twin System (DTS) delivers three core services: 1) **Data Monitoring** – real‑time visualization of vehicle state data, projected path, and surrounding object tracks via a user interface; data can be saved and exported to generate detailed reports. 2) **Computation Offloading** – the ACC algorithm (lead‑vehicle identification and desired‑speed calculation) is executed on cloud infrastructure and the resulting control commands are transmitted back to the vehicle, reducing on‑board ECU load. 3) **Remote Management** – operators can enable/disable ACC, set desired speed parameters, and issue emergency‑braking commands from a remote location through a low end‑to‑end latency channel. These services are designed for operators (monitoring and control) and developers (debugging and data collection).
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers: The DTS is implemented on cloud computing platforms to host the ACC offloading service, enabling compute‑intensive processing beyond the vehicle’s ECU capacity. A low end‑to‑end latency communication link (e.g., vehicle‑to‑cloud 5G or dedicated LTE) connects the physical twin to the digital twin, ensuring safety‑critical timing constraints. The system incorporates built‑in data‑collection and reporting subsystems that capture telemetry for analysis, and the UI is built following community best practices for digital twin presentation.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_time_scale: The Digital Twin operates with a strict clock synchronization requirement of ≤ 100 ms between the physical vehicle sensors/actuators and the digital simulation, as mandated by safety‑critical vehicular standards and hardware limitations. Computation offloading relies on this shared notion of time to ensure consistent state updates, and the twin’s services adhere to sub‑100 ms latency budgets for real‑time decision making.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: Architecture follows ISO 23247 Reference Architecture. Functional Elements (FEs) are mapped onto ROS 2 packages, each containing one or more ROS 2 nodes that implement the twin logic. The system is distributed across three logical entities: 1) Physical Twin Entity (hosted on on‑board vehicle hardware, managing raw sensor data, point‑cloud clustering, actuation); 2) Digital Twin Entity (hosted on edge/edge core, responsible for data processing, state estimation, control algorithms, data storage); 3) User Entity (providing a User Interface FE split into 3D Visualization and Control Panel). Each FE communicates via ROS 2 DDS middleware, providing publish‑subscribe message passing, whereas point‑cloud clustering may be implemented using PCL and tracking with OpenCV. Resource management is achieved by deploying each FE to specific compute nodes, enabling realistic scaling and fault isolation. The absence of a dedicated FE for the Data Storage component illustrates the flexible mapping approach permitted by ISO 23247.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: The Digital Twin incorporates three primary computational modules: (1) an Adaptive Cruise Control (ACC) system that monitors inter‑vehicle distance and relative velocity to compute the desired following speed of the ego vehicle; (2) a Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking module that processes raw LiDAR point clouds, applies the DBSCAN clustering algorithm to segment free space and moving obstacles, and then employs a Kalman filter to track the positions and velocities of identified objects; and (3) a Data Storage component that consolidates tracked object trajectories and the ego vehicle’s state (position, velocity, pose) into a SQL database for persistence and subsequent analytics. The data pipeline flows from the physical sensors (LiDAR, depth camera) to the clustering module, then to the tracking and storage components, establishing a one‑to‑one relationship between the sensor streams and the corresponding model outputs.
extracted_characteristics.physical_acting_components: The actuation subsystem is driven by a Vedder Electronic Speed Controller (ESC) that converts commanded wheel speed into voltage signals for the drive motor, and a high‑torque steering servo motor that controls lateral vehicle motion by adjusting steering wheel angle.  Both actuators are powered and commanded by low‑current analog outputs from the Data Collection and Device Control Entity, with built‑in safety interlocks that terminate actuation in the event of fault conditions such as over‑speed or over‑steer.
extracted_characteristics.physical_sensing_components: Sensing is provided by a Hokuyo UST‑10LX LiDAR, which emits a laser beam sweep to capture range data within its field of view, and an Intel DeepSense D435i depth camera, which delivers dense 3‑D point‑cloud data via stereo vision.  The LiDAR data stream is forwarded to the Point‑Cloud Clustering & Tracking functional entity, while the depth camera data feeds the same entity for object segmentation and tracking.  No additional sensor specifications (e.g., frequency, USB interfaces) are disclosed in the document.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction:
Raw LiDAR point‑cloud is captured by the on‑board LiDAR and immediately forwarded to the Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking component (the Data Pre‑Processing FE). This component segments the raw points into object clusters, maintains temporal tracks across successive scans, and produces object‑track, vehicle state and projected driving‑path streams. These sensor outputs, along with actuator status and vehicle dynamics, are then supplied to the ISO‑23247 Data Translation FE, which serialises the data, aligns heterogeneous message types and performs unit conversions. The harmonised payloads are distributed within the vehicle over a ROS2 middleware network and exposed to the external Digital Twin services over a Wi‑Fi link. The 3D Visualization FE consumes the pre‑processed streams to render a real‑time vehicle‑environment model.

All communication between SW components occurs inside a ROS2 network; inter‑entity communication (vehicle ↔ DT) takes place over the vehicle’s Wi‑Fi link.

No explicit transmission frequencies are documented; however, the LiDAR and vehicle state data are delivered continuously as soon as they become available, enabling real‑time visualization.

Data quality assurance is achieved by the Data Pre‑Processing FE’s clustering and tracking algorithms and the Data Translation FE’s unit‑normalisation and message‑type alignment, ensuring that the DT receives consistent, validated, high‑level data.

Data integration is performed by the Data Translation FE, which aggregates the heterogeneous streams into a unified representation that is subsequently forwarded to downstream services such as reporting and analytics.
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction: Control flow from the digital twin to the physical twin is mediated by the Actuation FE component. Commands such as ACC enable/disable, desired ACC speed adjustment, and emergency‑brake activation are generated by the Control Panel UI (user entity) and serialized by the Data Translation component. The resulting messages are forwarded over the ROS 2 network as topics to Actuation nodes linked to the vehicle motor, steering, and servo actuators. The document does not describe explicit authentication or validation mechanisms; confirmation of command execution is inferred from periodic feedback of updated vehicle state (speed, position, steering angle) published by the Vehicle State FE back to the digital twin. No conflict resolution strategy is documented.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: All intra‑vehicle software components communicate using a ROS2 middleware network. Inter‑entity communication—between the vehicle (Physical Twin Entity) and the Digital Twin Entity—is performed over the vehicle’s Wi‑Fi link. The system must satisfy a network latency requirement of at most 100 ms from the vehicle to the DT Entity (Requirement R7). The exact transport layer protocol (e.g., TCP or UDP) is not specified beyond the use of the Wi‑Fi network. Bandwidth, latency guarantees beyond the 100 ms target, redundancy, reliability or security protocols (such as encryption or authentication) are not mentioned in the documents.
extracted_characteristics.dt_hosting_deployment: Deployment occurs at the edge: each logical entity (Physical Twin, Digital Twin, User Entity) is instantiated on a distinct hardware node, as described in Section IV.B. The primary compute node hosting the Digital Twin services runs on an NVIDIA Jetson Orin Nano, providing GPU‑accelerated point‑cloud processing and ROS 2 execution. Data Storage and Reporting FEs persist event logs and telemetry into a SQL relational database, presumably on a separate local or cloud server. The document provides no explicit scalability, fail‑over, or cloud‑integration details; deployment is therefore constrained to the referenced on‑premise edge platform and the associated databases.
extracted_characteristics.standardization: The Digital Twin system complies with ISO 23247 – Digital twin framework for manufacturing, specifically Part 2 – Reference Architecture. This standard prescribes an entity‑based framework that groups functionality into four main entities: the Observable Manufacturing Element (OME) representing the physical asset; the Data Collection and Device Control Entity, which gathers data from and issues control commands to the OME; the Digital Twin Entity, which provides the digital representation of the OME; and the User Entity, which offers user interfaces for monitoring and interacting with the Digital Twin. Each of these entities is further decomposed into functional entities (FEs) responsible for tasks such as data transformation, actuating the OME, providing human‑interpretable formats, or interfacing with the user. ISO 23247 does not mandate specific implementation mechanisms; however, the document cites OPC Unified Architecture as a potential protocol for integration. The system’s architecture, as described, follows the ISO 23247 reference architecture, ensuring that the DT can interoperate within the broader industrial digital twin ecosystem and adhere to the prescribed standards for manufacturing digital twins.

block_1_docs_retrieved:
_A. Context_


DT technology enables an array of benefits for ACC through
off-loading any critical or non-critical computation for which
resource-constrained electronic control units (ECU) are typically not sufficient. To implement the ACC-DT, we develop
the following three services through a DT.
_1) Data Monitoring:_ Monitoring ACC-relevant data flowing through a vehicle is crucial for tracking system performance. The DT presented in this case will contain a user
interface (UI) allowing a user to view, save, and generate
reports of vehicle state data. Additionally, the UI will display
real-time vehicle state data, the projected driving path, and
detected object tracks in the vehicle’s proximity.
_2) Computation Offloading:_ A DT can alleviate the computational burden placed on resource-constrained on-board
hardware by allocating compute to better-suited equipment,
e.g., in the cloud. In this case study, we demonstrate this
functionality by offloading only the ACC system from the
vehicle to the DT. The ACC system determines the lead
vehicle, then computes a desired speed based on the distance
to the lead vehicle, finally sending the desired speed back to
the vehicle where the actuation is carried out.


(a) Base hardware: teleoperated vehicle retrofitted with advanced
sensor and control technology



(b) Main hardware layers of the physical twin: lower and upper level
chassis, and the autonomy elements



Fig. 1: The physical twin: a 1/10 [th] -scale autonomous vehicle



_3) Remote Management:_ The ability to remotely manage
ADAS systems enables the development, testing, and operation
of these systems from a different geographical location than
the physical twin. In addition to displaying real-time state data,
the UI will also enable the management and operation of ACC
remotely. This includes enabling/disabling ACC, setting the
desired speed, and sending an emergency braking command.
To implement these services, the DT must support low
end-to-end latency communication with the vehicle, contain
data collection and reporting capabilities, and more—all while
respecting the safety-critical nature of the system. For convenience and following community best practices, we refer
to the collection of the DT Entity, physical and middleware
components, and the UI as the digital twin system (DTS).
The users of our system can be grouped into two classes:
operators and developers. Operators use the DT to monitor
and remotely manage ACC, and developers use the DT to
troubleshoot during development using the data collection and
monitoring capabilities. In the _Liberty to act vs. Ability to act_
taxonomy from David and Bork [30], this DTS falls under the
Fully Autonomous Digital Twin, with the Human-in-the-Loop.


_B. Physical Twin_

The description perfectly aligns with the three services defined in the text: Data Monitoring (visualization, reporting), Computation Offloading (ACC algorithm in cloud), and Remote Management (enable/disable ACC, speed control, emergency braking). It correctly identifies the target users (operators and developers) and their specific uses. Quotes: '1) Data Monitoring: ... user interface (UI) allowing a user to view, save, and generate reports', '2) Computation Offloading: ... offloading only the ACC system ... to the DT', '3) Remote Management: ... enabling/disabling ACC, setting the desired speed, and sending an emergency braking command', 'operators ... developers'

The description correctly mentions the cloud infrastructure for offloading and the necessity of low end-to-end latency communication, though the text specifically mentions Wi-Fi rather than 5G/LTE. It accurately includes the data collection/reporting subsystems and the UI design based on community best practices. Quotes: 'allocate compute to better-suited equipment, e.g., in the cloud', 'communication ... occurs over a Wi-Fi network', 'data collection and reporting capabilities', 'following community best practices'

block_2_docs_retrieved:
coordination between devices. To enable computation
offloading, devices must share the same notion of time.
Many standards mandate stricter clock synchronization
than 100 ms for safety-critical vehicular applications. We
require 100 ms accuracy due to hardware limitations.


_B. Architecture_


We draw on the ISO 23247 RA in the architectural design
phase. First, we identify key software (SW) components
(Tab. I) and their relationships to each other, and to the sensors
and actuators of the physical twin (Fig. 2).
Next, we map the SW components onto FEs and implement
them. To enable their execution on the vehicle, we implement
each FE as an individual ROS2 package. Each package contains one or more ROS2 nodes implementing the functionality
provided by the FE. This mapping helps organize the SW
components by the entity-based structure of ISO 23247.
User-facing components are organized into the _User Entity_,
within which the singular _User Interface FE_ is instantiated
in the _3D Visualization_ and _Control Panel_ components. SW
components that implement data management and control
mechanisms are organized into the _Digital Twin Entity_ . Data
pre-processing, actuation, and parts of the device communication functionality are organized into the _Physical Twin_
_Entity_ . The existence of this entity marks a departure from
the ISO 23247 RA. Most similar to the Physical Twin Entity
in the standard is the OME, which hosts resource-specific
functionality. By contrast, in our design, the Physical Twin
Entity reflects the inherent resources of the twinned system (e.g., compute, sensors, actuators) and can host general
automotive functionality, such as _Point Cloud Clustering &_
_Tracking_ . Considering that each entity has been deployed on
a different hardware component, such deviations from the
standard are substantial as they may impact the ownership of
the functionality and the engineering process of the DTS.
Not every ISO 23247 FE was required for our DTS. At the
same time, the _User Interface FE_ has been split into two subcomponents: visualization and control panel. Finally, one SW
component, _Data Storage_ did not have a naturally aligned FE.


**Physical Twin Entity*** **Digital Twin Entity** **User Entity**

block_3_docs_retrieved:
In addition, a demonstration of the DT is publicly available. [2]


II. B ACKGROUND AND RELATED WORK


_A. The ISO 23247 standard and reference architecture_


In 2021, the International Standards Organization (ISO)
published the _ISO 23247 – Digital twin framework for manu-_
_facturing_ standard [10] that defines a development framework
for DTs. The standard is organized into four parts: 1) Overview
and general principles, 2) Reference architecture, 3) Digital
representation of physical manufacturing elements, and 4)
Information exchange. For our purposes, Part 2 is the most
relevant as it defines an entity-based RA, with each entity is
responsible for implementing a part of the DT’s functionality.
The _Observable Manufacturing Element (OME)_ is any item
that has an observable physical presence or operation in
manufacturing, e.g., equipment, facilities, products, personnel,
processes, etc. The _Data Collection and Device Control Entity_
is responsible for collecting data from, and controlling the
OME. The _Digital Twin Entity_ digitally represents the OME.
Finally, the _User Entity_ provides interfaces for users to interact
with the _Digital Twin Entity_, e.g., monitoring data or controlling the OME. Each entity is composed of _Functional Entities_
(FEs), which are smaller units responsible for a particular task
such as actuating the OME, transforming data into a humaninterpretable format, or interfacing with the user. The standard
does not give guidelines as to how FEs are to be implemented.
For example, one can choose to use OPC Unified Architecture

block_3_judge.reasoning: The extraction accurately details the three computational modules described in the source: ACC, Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking, and Data Storage. It correctly identifies algorithms (DBSCAN, Kalman filter) and data management tools (SQL database) explicitly listed in Table I and describes the data flow visible in Figure 2. Quotes: 'Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking Clusters raw LiDAR point clouds via DBSCAN, then tracks using a Kalman filter.', 'ACC Determines lead vehicle and computes following speed.', 'Data Storage Gathers object tracks and ego vehicle state into a SQL database.'

The extraction correctly identifies the 'Vedder Electronic Speed Controller' and its function to 'converts desired speed into motor voltage commands' as per Table I. However, it adds significant unsupported details: the text does not mention 'low-current analog outputs' or 'built-in safety interlocks that terminate actuation in the event of fault conditions such as over-speed or over-steer'. These hallucinations regarding the control interface and safety mechanisms reduce the score.

The extraction correctly lists the specific sensors found in Section III.B ('Hokuyo UST-10LX LiDAR', 'Intel Deepsense D435i Depth Camera') and correctly identifies the data flow to the 'Point-Cloud Clustering & Tracking' entity per Table I. It also correctly notes the absence of frequency or USB specifications in the document. However, it relies on outside knowledge to describe the sensor mechanisms ('emits a laser beam sweep', 'via stereo vision'), as these phrases are not present in the source text.

The document mentions general concepts of high-fidelity and sets system requirements for latency and clock synchronization (R7, R8) to support real-time operation, but it does not describe specific model fidelity requirements, detailed validation and verification methods, uncertainty sources, error correction mechanisms, or quality assurance processes as requested by the characteristic definition. The text states 'DTs are real-time and high-fidelity computational reflections' as a definition, and notes 'validate compliance with requirements [22]' as a citation, without providing the technical descriptions required.

II. B ACKGROUND AND RELATED WORK


_A. The ISO 23247 standard and reference architecture_


In 2021, the International Standards Organization (ISO)
published the _ISO 23247 – Digital twin framework for manu-_

relevant as it defines an entity-based RA, with each entity is

The _Observable Manufacturing Element (OME)_ is any item
